texts.orig: The  membrane  functions  as  a  selective  barrier,  transporting  the desired substances while other substances are rejected (Qiu et al., 2019). In  recent  years,  membrane  separation  technology  (MST)  has  gained widespread attention due to its potential application in energy demand and sustainable green development (Zeng et al., 2022). MST offers significant advantages like high separation accuracy, high operability, low energy  requirements,  low  cost,  and  low  carbon  footprint  (Ali  et  al., 2020; Ang et al., 2020; Zuo et al., 2021). Advances in membrane engineering  have  promoted  desalination  and  gas  separation.  Reverse osmosis (RO) membrane desalination technology is crucial to solving human survival challenges. Current commercial RO membranes used in the desalination industry are mainly thin film composite (TFC) membranes, containing a relatively dense selective layer and a porous support  layer  (Li  et  al.,  2021;  Lim  et  al.,  2021a).  The  selective  layer  is typically composed of polyamides formed via interfacial polymerization (IP)  reaction  (Yao  et  al.,  2020).  Moreover,  the  microstructure  of  the polyamide layer can be adjusted to apply in different membrane separation processes, for instance, nanofiltration (NF) (Li et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2020; Weng et al., 2020), forward osmosis (FO) (Suwaileh et al., 2020;  Yu  et  al.,  2020). texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane separation technology  has  shown  great  potential  as  an  indispensable  application  in  the  growing energy demand. Desalination and gas separation is challenging membrane separation processes; among them, membrane materials play a crucial role. Many modern nanomaterials, such as inorganic-organic mixed nanomaterials (covalent/metal-organic frameworks (COFs/MOFs)), can shape specific channels for conveying and separating target molecules due to their structural tunability. By combining novel nanomaterials with traditional polymeric materials, new opportunities have arisen, significantly improving performance and reducing energy consumption. This review aims to generalize the advantages and limitations of varying membrane materials, including organic and inorganic materials. Furthermore, emerging nanomaterials and new mixed matrix materials are discussed from different perspectives. Finally, we proposed to combine outstanding membranes with various novel materials to enhance energy-efficient membrane materials ' commercial applications.
texts.text: Membrane separation technology  has  shown  great  potential  as  an  indispensable  application  in  the  growing energy demand. Desalination and gas separation is challenging membrane separation processes; among them, membrane materials play a crucial role. Many modern nanomaterials, such as inorganic-organic mixed nanomaterials (covalent/metal-organic frameworks (COFs/MOFs)), can shape specific channels for conveying and separating target molecules due to their structural tunability. By combining novel nanomaterials with traditional polymeric materials, new opportunities have arisen, significantly improving performance and reducing energy consumption. This review aims to generalize the advantages and limitations of varying membrane materials, including organic and inorganic materials. Furthermore, emerging nanomaterials and new mixed matrix materials are discussed from different perspectives. Finally, we proposed to combine outstanding membranes with various novel materials to enhance energy-efficient membrane materials ' commercial applications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theoretically,  a  desirable  membrane  is  expected  to  allow  the required substances to flow at the highest rate, and it should entirely reject  unwanted  substances.  However,  there  is  a  trade-off  between permeability  and  selectivity  in  the  majority  of  membrane  materials, whereby  an  improvement  in  permeability  leads  to  a  degradation  in selectivity. This phenomenon was first found in gas separation membranes and other dense polymeric membranes (RO, NF, and FO membranes).  (Liu  et  al.,  2021;  Wang  et  al.,  2022).  For  example,  in  RO membranes,  the  trade-off  is  also  embodied  as  the  water  flux,  salt rejection rate, water permeability coefficient, or water-salt selectivity. Membrane-based trade-offs lead to low desalination performance, low permeate performance, and severe biofouling (Ali et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020). Many researchers addressed this issue by adding nanomaterials and surface modifications (Xu et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2018). And the modified membranes have comparable properties to commercial membranes but have poor controllability. Materials are vital to membrane separation  and  affect  separation  performance,  operational  flexibility, method layout, and eventual suitability of the membrane. Therefore, it is urgent  to  analyze  existing  membrane  materials ' characteristics  and develop novel ones.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  recent  years,  many  researchers  have  provided  profound  summaries and insights into the development of separation membranes (Ali et al., 2021; Raza et al., 2022; Sanaeepur et al., 2022). However, there lacks a comprehensive overview and outlook for different membrane materials  for  desalination  and  gas  separation  applications.  Here  we intend  to  summarize  the  characteristics  of  membrane  materials  and advances  to  promote  the  practical  separation  applications  of  novel materials. Section 2 presents a concise description of the classification and properties of organics, inorganic, mixed matrix (MM), and novel materials and highlights typical novel material structures for separation applications.  Section  3  introduces  recent  advances  in  theoretical research on the utilization of various membrane materials, and further summarizes  the  advantages  and  limitations  of  membrane  materials. Finally, section 4 concludes with the emerging challenges, prospects, and opportunities.

texts.orig: 2. Types of membrane materials
texts.text: 2. Types of membrane materials

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane materials attracted  increasing  attention  as  the  core  of MST (Firouzjaei et al., 2020). The earliest separation membrane materials are cellulose and its derivatives. In recent years, various high-performance cellulose and polymeric organic polymer membrane materials have been developed (Rana et al., 2021). Inorganic membrane materials  such  as  metals,  metal  oxides  (Gu  et  al.,  2016),  ceramics (Kujawa et al., 2016), zeolites (Kim et al., 2013), porous glass (Phiri et al., 2019), graphene oxide (GO) (Chen et al., 2021), MXene (Meng et al., 2021), and organic-inorganic hybrid membrane materials, such as MOFs (Shu et al., 2022), as well as COFs, biomimetic materials and other materials have emerged (Fig. 1) (Raza et al., 2022). We divide membrane materials  based  on  the  nature  of  materials,  including  organic, inorganic, MM, and novel membrane materials.

texts.orig: top-down selective etching. The CVD approach enables the preparation of excellent two-dimensional (2D) MXenes with large lateral dimensions at a low defect level (Xu et al., 2015).
texts.text: top-down selective etching. The CVD approach enables the preparation of excellent two-dimensional (2D) MXenes with large lateral dimensions at a low defect level (Xu et al., 2015).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The majority of MXene-based membranes were designed using the top-down  selective  etching  of ' A ' species  from  the  MAX  phases (Mn + 1AXn).  MAX  phase  materials  consist  of  almost  closed  transition metal  (M).  And  octahedral  positions  are  populated  with  C/N  atoms, forming Mn + 1Xn layers and interspersed with main group element layers in multiple-layer stacks (Fig. 2a) (Anasori et al., 2017). The MAX phase is sustained by strong metallic bonds between M-A, rather than intermolecular forces,  making it difficult  to  peel  off  by  mechanical  shear alone (Naguib et al., 2014). These layers can be delaminated by stripping A-atoms. The M-A bond has higher reactivity than M-X bond. Once A from the Mn + 1AXn  stack  is  removed,  Mn + 1Xn  is  obtained,  and  the peeling  process  gets  more  accessible,  resulting  in  the  formation  of Mn + 1Xn nanosheets (called MXenes). Due to the strong binding energy of M-A bonds, selective etching is generally performed by intense etching agents.  Therefore,  it  cannot  severely  damage  the  2D  construction  of other  Mn + 1Xn stacks  (Fig.  2b).  In  the  etching  procedure,  MXene nanosheets  are  formed  naturally  at  a  specific  concentration,  and ultrasound-assisted peeling of Mn + 1Xn stacks can obtain a large amount of  MXenes,  which  can  be  considered  multilayer  MXenes  (Fig.  2c) (Malaki et al., 2019).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Stacked  MXene  nanosheets  showed  distinct  advantages  for  membrane  separations.  First,  MXene  nanosheets  have  good  dispersion  in aqueous solutions and other solutions (Maleski et al., 2017). 2D MXene membranes can be obtained via simple methods, such as extraction or coating. In addition, MXene membranes have been successfully developed  for  desalination  (Han  et  al.,  2017;  Kang  et  al.,  2017)  and  gas separation (Ding et al., 2018; L. Li et al., 2018) due to their hydrophilicity and adjustable layer spacing for water permeation and molecular rejection.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: GO :  GO,  as  a  monolayer  material,  expose  abundant  oxygenated functional  groups  on  the  surface  and  among  the  edges,  is  stable  in aqueous solutions as well as polar solvents, and is known as a highly hydrophilic material (Chen et al., 2009; Dikin et al., 2007). GO can be obtained at a low-cost from graphite by oxidation. (Dreyer et al., 2010; Schniepp et al., 2006). Hummers ' method is the most commonly used method for preparing GO. Oxidized graphite was oxidized by KMnO4 in concentrated sulfuric acid, and then followed by ultrasonic peeling to obtain GO (Hummers and Offeman, 1958). Later, various reactions have been  developed  to  improve  the  yield  and  decrease  toxic  emissions (Marcano et al., 2010; Tung et al., 2009).

Oxidized graphite was oxidized by KMnO4 in concentrated sulfuric acid, and then followed by ultrasonic peeling to obtain GO (Hummers and Offeman, 1958). Later, various reactions have been  developed  to  improve  the  yield  and  decrease  toxic  emissions (Marcano et al., 2010; Tung et al., 2009).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Graphene is only one atom in thickness and the delocalized electron clouds in the p orbitals hinder the gaps in the aromatic rings of graphene, making it impervious (Berry, 2013). The impermeability of graphene allows it to act as a barrier to the permeation of gases and liquids (Prasai et al., 2012). This particular property of graphene has greatly inspired researchers  to  explore  graphene  materials.  They  have  designed  graphene as nanoporous graphene (NG) sheets and stacked GOs and applied it to membrane separation (Perreault et al., 2015). NG membranes with precise nanometer-sized pores achieved membrane selectivity mainly through  size  rejection  and  electrostatic  exclusion  between  charged material and the pore. The interlayer distance between GO sheets in stacked GO membranes determines the pore diameter size. The superior selectivity of stacked GO membranes is achieved through size exclusion and electrostatic interaction, as well as the sorption of ions by GO flakes. Nair et al. (2012) found that during membrane filtration, the channels formed between the stacked GO layers allowed water to pass rapidly while rejecting other substances, effectively improving the permeability and selectivity of the membrane.

In addition, with their large  Hamaker  constants,  nanosheets  of  this  material  have  stronger molecular interactions with each other than GO. The strengthened interactions  between  adjacent  MoS2 nanosheets  have  a  more  stable laminar structure in the aqueous solution in contrast with laminated GO membranes  (Deng  et  al.,  2017;  Wang  et  al.,  2017).  Stacked  MoS2 nanosheets  typically  have  ~0.63  nm,  after  subtracting  the  atomic thickness  about  ~0.30  nm  (Wang  et  al.,  2017).  Theoretically,  a nano-channel of this dimension allows water molecules to permeate, excluding sodium and chloride ions, which gives a compelling choice to fabricate highly selective desalination membranes. As shown in Fig. 3, the lamellar 2D nanosheets are deposited on the porous support layers, forming a composite separation membrane. The interconnected nanochannels  between  the  2D  nanosheets  allow  rapid  water  permeation while rejecting other substances through a size-exclusion mechanism (Lu et al., 2020).

texts.orig: Although  2D  materials  are  widely  used  in  membrane  separation (such as GO, MoS2, and MXene), the differences in the materials ' characteristics lead to disparity in membrane separation. Among them, the synthesis  technology  of  GO  and  its  application  is  relatively  mature. However, GO swells in water, leading to poor mechanical and membrane stability of lamellar membranes. On the other hand, the absence of hydrophilic  makes  MoS2  membranes  more  stable  in  water  than  GO membranes (Wang and Mi, 2017). However, it is challenging to obtain well-dispersed monolayer MoS2  nanosheets. Compared to GO, MXene has better compatibility with polymers, which facilitates its application in composite membranes (Li et al., 2020). However, the easy degradation of MXene  under  humid conditions and harsh preparation conditions.

texts.orig: MOFs: MOFs are unique hybrid inorganic-organic nanoporous materials that form one-, two- or three-dimensional topologies by linking inorganic metal ions or groups with organic ligands (Raza et al., 2022). Recently,  MOF-based membranes have enjoyed significant growth in elaborate-designed and versatile MOFs (Li et al., 2015). The application of  MOF materials is progressively expanding to desalination and gas separation. Specifically, the composition and pore tunability properties of MOF played an outstanding role in desalination and gas separation (Kang et al., 2017). In addition, MOF ' s structure and properties play an essential part in terms of membrane separation performance, and Fig. 4 shows some typical structures of MOF.

In addition, MOF ' s structure and properties play an essential part in terms of membrane separation performance, and Fig. 4 shows some typical structures of MOF.

texts.orig: Currently,  the  membranes  presently  being  applied  for  primary desalination  treatments  confront  various  challenges,  such  as  low permeability (RO), low selectivity (NF), and internal concentration polarization (FO). As a result, seeking membrane materials to develop new membranes might overcome these challenges.
texts.text: Currently,  the  membranes  presently  being  applied  for  primary desalination  treatments  confront  various  challenges,  such  as  low permeability (RO), low selectivity (NF), and internal concentration polarization (FO). As a result, seeking membrane materials to develop new membranes might overcome these challenges.

texts.orig: COFs : COFs are novel materials consisting of periodic extensions and covalently  bound  crystalline  porous  network  structures  (Lyu  et  al., 2019). Compared to other materials, COFs have attracted more attention in the area of membrane separations due to their tunable structure, ease of modification, low density, and high specific surface area (Lohse and Bein, 2018). Thus, COFs have prepared MMMs, composite membranes, and stand-alone COF membranes (Wang et al., 2020).

texts.orig: The synthesis of suitable COF for membrane separation is further complicated because different applications require COF materials with different properties, requiring rational selection criteria when preparing COF-based membranes (Yuan et al., 2019). In particular, the physical diameter of membrane pores determines the application in technical areas such as RO, NF, UF, and MF. To some extent, choosing a COF material  with  a  defined  pore  diameter  is  important  for  membrane orientation applications. Furthermore, other properties such as chemical and physical stability, surface hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity, and surface charge also make a crucial impact on the ability of COF materials (Das et al., 2020). In summary, many variables in the selection of COF decide  the  future  separation  potential  and  application  areas  of  the membrane.  Therefore,  the  optimized  approach  to  constructing  ideal COF-based  membranes  featuring  high  permeation  selectivity  and perennial stability would be to rationally select COF based on a combination of various properties of COF.

texts.orig: Biomimetic  materials :  Biological  channels  with  excellent  water transport capacity characteristics inspired researchers to explore biomimetic membranes in the growth of desalination. The mechanism of action  of  biomimetic  membranes  is  similar  to  the  biological  water channels (aquaporins, AQPs) or synthetic water-based nano channels embedded in lipid bilayers  (Lim  et  al.,  2021b)  or  amphiphilic  block copolymers (Werber et al., 2016). Aquaporins are inherent proteins that control the transport of water throughout biological membranes (Lim et al., 2022). A broad range of them are found in body tissues and are involved in the efficient but passive transportation of water from one cell  membrane to another (Pendergast and Hoek, 2011). Aquaporins mediate  and  induce  water  transport  in  an  osmotic  pressure  gradient through fast selective diffusion (Agre, 2004). The amino acid sequence of  the  amino  and  carboxy  termini  of  aquaporin-1  (AQP1)  is  strictly symmetrical,  with  the  hollow  portion  of  each  monomeric  protein forming highly selective channels that allow only water molecules to be transported across the membrane while rejecting proteins and charged protons or other ions by electrostatic interactions (Bowen, 2006; Giwa et al., 2017).

The amino acid sequence of  the  amino  and  carboxy  termini  of  aquaporin-1  (AQP1)  is  strictly symmetrical,  with  the  hollow  portion  of  each  monomeric  protein forming highly selective channels that allow only water molecules to be transported across the membrane while rejecting proteins and charged protons or other ions by electrostatic interactions (Bowen, 2006; Giwa et al., 2017).

texts.orig: For RO membranes, the AQP is innovatively embedded in the conventional  PA  selective  layer  and  can  significantly  influence  the  rate water passes through the membrane. Theoretically, biomimetic membranes containing AQPs have high penetration and selectivity. Li et al. (2019) initially reported biomimetic RO membranes based on AQPs for desalination.  AQPs were embedded in proteoliposomes (Fig. 7a) and added to the aqueous phase of MPD, which was a similar way to the classical  IP  process  (Fig.  7b).  The  flow  rate  of  the  AQPs-based  biomimetic RO membrane increased by 100% relative to the traditional membrane. This is because water forms a single longitudinal column as it passes through the proteins ' channel and enters the curved and narrow channels. Intermolecular forces and polarities assist water in rotating at right angles through the narrow pathways, ultimately increasing water permeability.  However,  the  current  permeate  selectivity  of  the  biomimetic  membrane  is  much  lower,  in  particular,  the  reported  salt rejection was lower than expected (Giwa et al., 2017). It is hypothesized that the gap between observed experimental and simulated results of the active layer defect. To address this issue, Werber et al. (2016) presented an idea to construct a biomimetic layer (Fig. 7c) on the PA active layer surface  (RO  membrane)  rather  than  on  porous  support  layer  surface

texts.orig: 3.1. Applications in desalination
texts.text: 3.1. Applications in desalination

texts.label: text
texts.orig: High water fluxes and rejection  rates  are  essential  for  evaluating desalination membranes ' selectivity. The main driver for highly selective water desalination membranes is the osmotic pressure difference or chemical potential difference between different water qualities. Improving  water  recovery  and  reducing  the  brine  ratio  while  maintaining  low energy consumption requirements are common goals for achieving rapid membrane development. However, depending on water quality and end-use, different treatment options are required to achieve optimal  results  (Fig.  8)  (Zuo  et  al.,  2021).  For  instance,  separation membranes are needed to remove all contaminants and salt ions at the highest possible level in water-scarce coastal areas where seawater is the primary source of potable and industrial water.

texts.orig: 3.1.2. Desalination mechanisms
texts.text: 3.1.2. Desalination mechanisms

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The water transportation of separation for dense membranes follows the solution-diffusion theory (Raza et al., 2022). Selective transportation is  mainly  realized  by  two  mechanisms:  size  sieving  and  electrostatic repulsion.  With  the  incorporation  of  nanomaterials,  different  mechanisms may co-exist simultaneously, depending on the pore size of the filler material. For example, as the nanochannel sizes decrease to the molecular scale, the dominant mass transport behavior changes from interface  effects  to  molecular  sieving  to  solution-diffusion  (size  is decreasing order) (Goh et al., 2016). Li et al. (2020) developed a novel thin  film  nanocomposite  (TFN)  membrane  by  simultaneously  introducing two nano-fillers of different sizes. It not only improves the water permeability but also obtained excellent separation performance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The separation mechanism for membranes prepared from 2D materials  is  the  same,  except  that  the  densified  PA  layer  allows  selective separation, and the nanosheets also have the function of selective mass transport  (Safaei  et  al.,  2020).  In  2D  materials,  desalination  by  size repulsion is implemented by selective separation through nanopores of a specific size on a single layer of nanosheets (Fig. 9a and b) or by stacking nanosheets  together  and  forming  nanochannels  in  their  intervals (Fig. 11d and e) (You et al., 2016). Smaller-sized water are allowed to pass  through  the  nanopores,  while  larger-sized  hydrated  ions  are rejected.  The  electrostatic  repulsion  effect  means  that  the  charged nanosheet surface prevents the passage of hydrated ions (Zhang et al., 2019). The electrostatic effect is vital in the selective transport of 2D stacked  membranes  and  monolayer  nanoporous  membranes.  In  this process, the charges on the pore boundaries (Fig. 9c) or the boundaries and surfaces of nanosheets (Fig. 11f) prevent the passage of ions through electrostatic interactions.

texts.orig: The primary membrane currently used for desalination is the TFC membrane, and researchers have devoted themselves to enhancing its attractiveness.  In  addition,  they  contributed  to  improving  separation performance. Typically, TFC membranes are composed of the PA layer formed via IP reaction on the support layer. Therefore, the researchers attempted to enhance the separation capacity of TFC membranes by altering the support layer, PA active layer, and surface modification, respectively.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Applications in gas separation
texts.text: 3.2. Applications in gas separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The separation and utilization of gases are relevant to all dimensions of  human life,  from  the  living  environment  to  energy  and  industrial manufacturing. For instance, the separation and conversion of carbon dioxide (CO2) is essential for achieving global carbon neutrality targets and mitigating global warming; the purification and storage of hydrogen (H2) and methane (CH4) is essential for the spread of green fuels; and the control  of  toxic  gases  like  carbon  monoxide  (CO)  and  sulfur  dioxide (SO2) is essential for reducing emissions pollution (Y. Li et al., 2018). A typical  gas  separation  process  is  similar  to  the  desalination  process, where desired gas is permeated rapidly through the membrane, and the other  gases  are  rejected.  However,  developing  gas  separation  membranes is more challenging than desalination because of the tiny differences between gas-phase particle sizes (Usman et al., 2019).

texts.orig: For example, for the production of H2  from syngas, the separation membrane is usually encapsulated in a hollow fiber module, as shown in Fig. 11 (Qiu et al., 2019). Hollow fibers usually have porous walls and are  covered  with  a  selective  skin  layer  to  allow  rapid  passage  of small-sized  gases.  Under  the  pressure-driven  force,  the  raw  gas  is squeezed into the hollow fibers, the object gas flows through the selective layer as a penetration flow, and the desired gas is finally separated, leaving the other gases to be discharged through the end of the terminal (Jiao et al., 2011).

texts.orig: membranes, and dense membranes. Currently, there are two models of separation transport mechanisms, the pore flow, and solution diffusion models. Among them, porous membranes could be further divided into three  different  separation  mechanisms  based  on  pore  size  (Thornton et al., 2015). As shown in Fig. 12, the gas transport is dominated by convective  flow  with  pore  diameters  greater  than  1000  Å,  Knudsen diffusion with pore diameters ranging from 100 to 1000 Å, and molecular sieving mechanism with pore diameters about 5 -100 Å (Wang et al., 2017). When the pore size is below 5 Å, the separation membrane becomes  pretty  dense,  at  which  point  the  gas  separation  mechanism mainly  follows  the  solution  diffusion  model.  Under  the  present  circumstances,  gas  transport  through  the  membrane  is  not  a  purely pressure-controlled diffusion process but rather a process that passes through three steps in the membrane, including adsorbing, diffusing, and desorbing (Bielza et al., 2002; Wang et al., 2017).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Separation properties of gas separation membranes Materials for gas separation membranes are mainly polymers, such as cellulose, CA, polyether, polysulfone, and polyethersulfone. However, researchers  have  found  a  permeation  selectivity  trade-off  (Robeson upper  bound)  for  polymer  membranes.  Specifically,  highly  selective polymers exhibit low gas permeability, while highly permeable polymers exhibit low selectivity (Kamble et al., 2021). In latest years, a wide range of inorganic materials has been applied to fabricate gas separation membranes or as fillers to prepare organic-inorganic MMMs, aiming to exceed the upper limit line. As shown in Fig. 13, nanometer-thick 2D materials are becoming a basis for designing membranes with excellent properties (high gas permeation flux and selectivity) due to their unique nanostructures (Liu et al., 2018). Table 4 summarizes the performance of membranes of different materials for gas separation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Challenges and prospects
texts.text: 4. Challenges and prospects

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Desalination  and  gas  separation  is  among  the  more  important membrane separation processes. And recently, many efforts have been focused  on  enhancing  the  performance  of  membranes.  To  customize viable membranes for specific extraordinary fields, exploring and pursuing the polymer membrane in combination with novel materials with excellent performance and separation capabilities is urgent. Combining polymers  with  inorganic  materials  is  a  more  effective  solution  as  it ischallenging to adapt polymers ' chemistry and physical structure. As a result, a number of emerging materials have created new opportunities for combining with polymers. For example, MOFs, COFs, and MXene 2D nanomaterials have been extensively investigated and milestones have been achieved in polymer membrane performance.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusions
texts.text: 5. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Many  studies  have  demonstrated  the  excellent  performance  and challenges in different materials encountered for desalination and gas separation. In particular, new materials and hybrid matrix membranes of polymers have shown substantial improvements in permeate selectivity. However, translating from theoretical forecasts to experimental and eventually to practical industrial-scale applications is facing many significant  challenges.  These  challenges  involve  the  manufacture  of large-scale, high-performance, integrated-technology membrane modules of novel materials, academic applications of transport processes, and  regulation  of  pore  structure,  interlayer  stacking,  and  interfacial binding interactions with polymers. However, the exciting advantages offered  by  the  unique  pore  characteristics  of  new  materials  should motivate researchers to continue their efforts.
